turns.user_message: Look, doc, I don't really see the point of this. I've been smoking for years, and it's just part of who I am. I'm a mechanic, I work with my hands, and smoking is just something I do. It's not a big deal.
step2_rag_chunks: Keeping your hands busy with activities such as drawing, writing, or using a stress ball can help reduce the urge to smoke.
If someone offers you a cigarette, practice politely saying no and reminding yourself why you decided to quit.
Many people smoke out of habit during specific routines such as after meals, while drinking tea or coffee, or when talking with friends.

step4_session_phase.phase_instruction: You are in the EXPLORATION phase. You know the basics. Now gently explore what situations or emotions make them smoke more — triggers like stress, meals, social settings, boredom. Do NOT suggest any strategies yet. Just understand their situation.

step6_prompt: You are a warm, empathetic therapist helping patients overcome tobacco addiction.

- Do NOT use bullet points or dump multiple pieces of information at once
- First acknowledge what the patient said, then ask ONE follow-up question
- Use simple, conversational language — not clinical or formal

- Use your clinical judgment to decide what the patient needs right now

step2_rag_chunks: If you slip and smoke a cigarette, it does not mean you have failed. Many people attempt quitting several times before succeeding.
If someone offers you a cigarette, practice politely saying no and reminding yourself why you decided to quit.
Nicotine addiction is both physical and psychological. Addressing both aspects can improve success in quitting.

step2_rag_chunks: Relapse can happen during the quitting process. Instead of feeling discouraged, treat it as a learning experience and continue trying to quit.
Planning ahead for difficult situations such as parties or social events can help prevent relapse.

A relapse does not erase the progress made. The most useful question after a slip is not why did I fail but what was happening just before â€” what was the trigger, what was the situation. That specific information is what makes the next attempt more informed. Most people who successfully quit smoked again at some point during the process.
